question: Statements: All apples are fruits. All fruits are food. Conclusion: All apples are food.
solution: Apple ⊆ Fruit ⊆ Food, so All apples are food.

question: Statements: No cat is a dog. Conclusion: No dog is a cat.
solution: 'No A is B' is reversible, so 'No dog is a cat' follows.

question: Statements: Some pens are red. Conclusion: Some red (things) are pens.
solution: 'Some A are B' is reversible → 'Some B are A' follows.

question: Statements: All roses are flowers. Some flowers are red. Conclusion: Some roses are red.
solution: The red flowers need not be roses, so it does not follow.
trick: Overlap may avoid the rose region.

question: Statements: All A are B. Conclusion: All B are A.
solution: 'All' is NOT reversible; B may extend beyond A.

question: Statements: All dogs are animals. All animals need water. Conclusion: All dogs need water.
solution: Dog ⊆ Animal ⊆ (need water), so all dogs need water.

question: Statements: Some A are B. All B are C. Conclusions: I. Some A are C. II. All A are C. Which follows?
solution: The A that are B are also C, so Some A are C (I). Not all A need be C.

question: Statements: No book is a pen. All pens are blue. Conclusion: Some blue (things) are not books.
solution: Pens are blue and no pen is a book, so those blue pens are non-books → some blue are not books.

question: Statements: Some cats are white. Some white (things) are soft. Conclusion: Some cats are soft.
solution: Two 'Some' statements never give a definite conclusion.

question: Statements: All teachers are learned. Some learned are wise. Conclusions: I. Some teachers are wise. II. All wise are teachers. Which follows?
solution: The wise-learned need not be teachers (I fails); II is clearly not implied. Neither follows.
trick: Some+All from the wrong end gives nothing.

question: Statements: All P are Q. No Q is R. Conclusion: No P is R.
solution: P is inside Q and Q is separate from R, so P is separate from R.
trick: Inside a set that avoids R → avoids R.

question: Statements: Some A are B. No B is C. Conclusion: Some A are not C.
solution: The A that are B cannot be C (No B is C), so those A are not C.

question: Statements: All X are Y. Conclusions: I. Some Y are X. II. Some X are Y. Which follows?
solution: All X are Y implies some X are Y (II) and by reversal some Y are X (I). Both follow.

question: Statements: No A is B. Conclusions: I. Some A are not B. II. Some B are not A. Which follows?
solution: If no A is B, then every A is not B (so some A are not B) and every B is not A. Both follow (assuming A and B exist).

question: Statements: Some A are B. Some B are C. All C are D. Conclusion: Some A are D.
solution: The chain breaks at two 'Some' links; A need not connect to C or D.
trick: Two 'Some' still don't chain, even with a later 'All'.

question: Statements: All A are B. Some B are C. Conclusions: I. Some A are C. II. Some C are not A. Which is the 'either-or' pair status?
solution: The C that overlap B may or may not be in A, so neither I nor II is guaranteed.
trick: Overlap position is free → neither forced.

question: Statements: All singers are artists. No artist is a robot. Conclusion: No singer is a robot.
solution: Singers ⊆ artists, and artists exclude robots, so singers exclude robots.
trick: Subset of a robot-free set is robot-free.

question: Statements: Some A are B. All A are C. Conclusion: Some C are B.
solution: The A that are B are also C, so those C are B → Some C are B.

question: Statements: All A are B. All C are B. Conclusion: Some A are C.
solution: A and C both sit inside B but need not overlap each other.

question: Statements: Some A are B. Conclusions: I. Some A are not B. II. All A are B. Regarding these two, which is correct?
solution: 'Some A are B' allows either all A to be B or some A not B; neither I nor II is forced.
trick: 'Some' doesn't decide the rest of A.
